title-group.article-title: Gut Microbial Signatures in Long COVID: Potential Biomarkers and Therapeutic Targets

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/002wfgr58grid.484628.40000 0001 0943 2764Infectious Disease Research Center, Citizen’s Health Bureau, Seoul Metropolitan Government, Seoul, Republic of Korea
2https://ror.org/002wfgr58grid.484628.40000 0001 0943 2764Department of Food and Drug, Seoul Metropolitan Government Research Institute of Public Health and Environment, Seoul, Republic of Korea
3https://ror.org/04h9pn542grid.31501.360000 0004 0470 5905Department of Biomedical Sciences, College of Medicine, Seoul National University, Seoul, Republic of Korea
4https://ror.org/04h9pn542grid.31501.360000 0004 0470 5905Department of Microbiology and Immunology, College of Medicine, Seoul National University, Seoul, Republic of Korea
5https://ror.org/002nav185grid.415520.70000 0004 0642 340XDepartment of Family Medicine, Seoul Medical Center, Seoul, Republic of Korea

6https://ror.org/01z4nnt86grid.412484.f0000 0001 0302 820XDepartment of Internal Medicine and Healthcare Research Institute, Seoul National University Hospital Healthcare System Gangnam Center, Seoul, Republic of Korea
7https://ror.org/04h9pn542grid.31501.360000 0004 0470 5905Department of Internal Medicine and Liver Research Institute, Seoul National University College of Medicine, Seoul, Republic of Korea

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, adaptation, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if changes were made. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article's Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article's Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Following severe acute respiratory syndrome-coronavirus-2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection, symptoms can persist for more than 12 weeks in over 10% of patients in a condition known as long coronavirus disease (COVID). Gut microbiota dysbiosis is correlated with long COVID, but the specific relationship between long COVID and the gut microbiome remains unclear. Here, we aimed to investigate connections between the gut microbiota and long COVID severity.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Fecal samples were collected from 31 patients with long COVID, 14 with COVID-19 but not long COVID, and 23 healthy controls. The mean interval between COVID-19 diagnosis and sample collection was 65.5 (range: 13.0–110.3) weeks for patients with long COVID and 74.8 (range: 50.7–110.4) weeks for positive control group. Gut microbiota composition was analyzed using 16S rRNA gene sequencing. Patient-reported outcomes were used to comprehensively assess long COVID symptom severity.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Symptom severity was higher in patients with severe initial infections and significantly correlated with serum triglyceride, fasting blood glucose, and high-density lipoprotein-cholesterol levels. Results showed distinct microbial profiles in patients with long COVID. Leuconostoc, Actinomyces, and Granulicatella were significantly enriched, and they accurately distinguished patients with long COVID from controls, indicating their potential as long COVID biomarkers. Particular gut bacteria were significantly correlated with certain systemic, gastrointestinal, otolaryngologic, and visual symptoms. Parabacteroides, Eubacterium ventriosum group, and Rothia abundance was correlated with blood biomarkers that influence long COVID development, including total and low-density lipoprotein cholesterol and basophil levels.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: The gut microbial signature of patients with long COVID differed from that of healthy controls. Certain microbial genera showed significant differences between patients with long COVID and controls, suggesting potential as preliminary biomarkers and therapeutic targets for long COVID pending validation in larger studies.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1007/s40121-025-01167-6.

custom-meta.meta-value: © Springer Healthcare Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2025

tr.td: Gut microbiota dysbiosis is known to be correlated with long COVID, but the specific relationship between long COVID and the gut microbiome remains unknown.
Here, we aimed to investigate the relationship between the gut microbiota and long COVID severity.

bold.italic: What was learned from this study?

tr.td: The gut microbial signatures of patients with long COVID differed from those of healthy controls.
Further validation of these findings could aid in establishing the potential of the gut microbiota as a diagnostic biomarker and therapeutic target for long COVID.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The recent coronavirus disease-2019 (COVID-19) pandemic, caused by the novel severe acute respiratory syndrome-coronavirus-2 (SARS-CoV-2), has resulted in a global health emergency [1]. Beyond the initial phase of the disease, there has been an increasing focus on the long-term effects experienced by many survivors. These lingering symptoms, often collectively referred to as “long COVID” or post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection, can persist for several weeks or even months after the initial infection [2]. According to the UK’s Office for National Statistics, 13.7% of patients with COVID-19 experienced symptoms for over 12 weeks following the initial infection [3]. Similarly, a survey by the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention estimated that 14.3% of patients with COVID-19 developed long COVID [4]. Risk factors for long COVID include smoking, obesity, and various comorbidities, which may contribute to chronic symptomatology through mechanisms beyond immune modulation [5, 6]. Long COVID is characterized by a range of symptoms such as chronic fatigue, shortness of breath, cough, chest pain, palpitations, headache, sleep disorders, diarrhea, skin rash, hair loss, and memory and concentration impairment [7]. The diverse array of symptoms and their persistence have made diagnosing, managing, and treating long COVID challenging.

Recent research has been shedding light on potential contributing factors to long COVID, particularly focusing on the role of the gut microbiome [8]. The human gut microbiota, which consists of many microorganisms, is essential for maintaining a healthy balance in the body. It plays a critical role in functions such as regulating the immune system and metabolism as well as protecting against pathogens [9, 10]. Imbalances in the gut microbiota, known as dysbiosis, are associated with various chronic conditions, including metabolic disorders, autoimmune diseases, and mental health issues [11]. There is growing interest in the idea that gut dysbiosis may be central to the development and progression of long COVID, as emerging evidence suggests that SARS-CoV-2 infection can cause considerable changes in gut microbial communities [12]. For example, reductions in beneficial genera such as Faecalibacterium and Bifidobacterium have been reported, along with increases in opportunistic pathobionts such as Corynebacterium, Enterococcus, and Streptococcus, which are associated with chronic inflammatory conditions and may contribute to long COVID symptomatology [13].

The gut–lung axis establishes a crucial two-way communication pathway between the gut and the respiratory system, playing an integral role in maintaining respiratory health [14]. Imbalances in the gut microbiota are associated with compromised lung immunity and may contribute to the onset of respiratory conditions, including those exhibited during long COVID [15, 16]. Moreover, the gut–brain axis represents another important pathway, potentially contributing to specific neurological and psychological symptoms experienced by patients with long COVID [17, 18]. Although numerous studies have linked the severity of COVID-19 to the amount of SARS-CoV-2 virus in respiratory samples, the association between viral load in stool samples and severe COVID-19 remains the subject of debate [19, 20].

In this study, we aimed to explore the gut microbial changes linked to long COVID symptoms. We obtained patient-reported outcomes (PROs) through questionnaires and blood tests and analyzed the gut microbiome of participants on the basis of 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing to identify critical microbial signatures of patients with long COVID. This research could contribute to our understanding of long COVID and open new avenues for diagnosis and treatment.

sec.title: Study Design and Participants
sec.p: The inclusion criterion of age ≥ 40 years was established on the basis of the higher prevalence and severity of long COVID reported in older populations [21] and participants aged ≥ 40 years were recruited between August 2021 and October 2023. Among the patients who visited the Long COVID Clinic at Seoul Medical Center, 31 patients were recruited to the long COVID group. Volunteers without long COVID were recruited to the control groups. Among them, individuals who had a confirmed record of COVID-19 diagnosis (positive reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) test result for SARS-CoV-2) at the local healthcare centers via the COVID-19 information management system of the Korea Disease Control and Prevention Agency (a national agency of the Republic of Korea) were defined as positive RT-PCR controls (PCON group; 14 participants), whereas individuals without a record of COVID-19 were defined as negative RT-PCR controls (NCON group; 23 people). PCON and NCON participants were recruited through two public hospitals (Seoul Medical Center and Seoul National University Hospital) and one local government facility (Infectious Disease Research Center, Citizens’ Health Bureau) in Seoul, Republic of Korea. This study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of the Seoul Medical Center and Seoul National University Hospital (approval nos. SEOUL 2023–02-005 and 2011–202-1178). Informed consent was obtained from all study participants. The study was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki of 1964 and its later amendments.

We collected blood and stool samples for analyzing blood markers and microbiome, respectively. The average interval from COVID-19 diagnosis to the collection of fecal samples was 65.5 (range: 13.0–110.3) weeks for participants in the long COVID group and 74.8 (range: 50.7–110.4 ) weeks for those in the PCON group (data not shown). At the time of fecal sample collection, a survey was conducted to gather information on participants’ characteristics, as presented in Table 1. To examine 31 symptoms associated with long COVID, the degrees of worsening compared with those before COVID-19 were recorded on a 10-point scale based on patient judgment (PROs). Given the retrospective design of this study and the reliance on patient-reported outcome (PRO) questionnaires for assessing long COVID symptoms, we did not include the persistence of symptoms. The symptom questionnaire is presented in Supplementary Table S1. The severity of long COVID was evaluated by the total score of all symptoms, and the sum of each symptom score related to particular diseases was used to analyze the correlation with other biomarkers.Table 1
Demographic and clinical characteristics of control participants and patients with long COVID
Negative SARS-CoV-2 controls (NCON)Positive SARS-CoV-2 controls (PCON)Patients with long COVID (long COVID)
Number of individuals, n231431

*Age and body mass index (BMI) are shown as average value [min–max]

sec.title: Stool DNA Extraction
sec.p: Fecal samples were collected using disposable stool-sample containers (mediclin®, Republic of Korea; sterile, individual packs), immediately frozen at −20 °C. If more than 2 weeks had passed, they were kept at −80 °C until DNA extraction.

Microbial genomic DNA was extracted from the fecal samples using the E.Z.N.A.® Stool DNA Extraction Kit (Omega Bio-tek, USA), following the manufacturer’s instructions. DNA was eluted with 50 μL elution buffer, and its concentration and purity were analyzed using the NanoDrop One UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

sec.title: 16S rRNA Gene Sequencing and Bioinformatic Analyses
sec.p: We prepared 16S rRNA amplicons (V3–V4 region) according to the Illumina 16S metagenomic sequencing library preparation protocol. The V3–V4 region of the bacterial 16S rRNA was amplified using 341-F (5′-CCTACGGGNGGCWGCAG-3′)/805-R (5′-GACTACHVGGGTATCTAATCC-3′) primer set (Cosmogenetech, Daejeon, Republic of Korea) and OneTaq Hot Start 2X Master Mix (New England Biolabs, Ipswich, MA, USA). The amplicons were quantified using a GenNext NGS Library Quantification Kit (Toyobo, Osaka, Japan) according to the manufacturer’s instructions.

The libraries were sequenced by performing next-generation sequencing on the Illumina MiSeq system using 250 base pair (bp) paired-end reads (Illumina, USA). The resulting raw reads were analyzed using the QIIME2 platform (version 2020.08). Paired-end reads were trimmed and joined using default parameters. Denoising to derive amplicon sequence variants was performed using the DADA2 package [22, 23]. Taxonomic assignment of each amplicon sequence variant was conducted on the basis of the SILVA database (Release 132) using integrated analysis tools within QIIME 2 [24]. To determine α-diversity, Chao1, Simpson, and Shannon indices were calculated through plug-in within QIIME 2; the Chao1 index estimates the number of observed species (richness), and the Simpson and Shannon indices estimate both species richness and evenness. Non-metric multidimensional scaling plots, which visually represent the major bacterial compositional differences between samples, were generated using PRIMER version 7 (PRIMER-e, New Zealand). Analysis of compositions of microbiomes with bias correction (ANCOM-BC) was conducted through q2-composition plug-in of QIIME 2 (version 2024.04). For primary identification of microbial taxonomic groups that differed between groups in each comparison condition, random forest was used in the web-based platform MicrobiomeAnalyst 2.0 (https://www.microbiomeanalyst.ca/) [25]. We considered 500 decision trees and identical training and testing data to determine the classification features for the final model.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Data were analyzed and visualized using GraphPad Prism 9 (GraphPad, USA), PRIMER 7, and R 3.6.3 (R Foundation for Statistical Computing, Austria). The area under the curve (AUC) values were calculated using SPSS 24 (IBM SPSS, USA). Continuous variables of demographic features are expressed as mean (standard deviation). Statistical significance was determined using the Mann–Whitney test, and correlations were analyzed using Spearman’s rank correlation coefficient. Results with p < 0.05 were considered significant.

sec.title: Demographic and Clinical Characteristics and Correlation between Long COVID Symptoms and Biomarkers
sec.p:
This study involved 31 patients with long COVID, 14 PCONs, and 23 NCONs. The demographic and clinical characteristics of the participants are presented in Table 1. In the long COVID group (n = 31), the mean age was 61.1 (range: 44–79) years; 16 (51.6%) were female individuals; and the mean BMI was 22.5 (range: 17.4–29.6) kg/m2 (Table 1). Among them, 11 participants (35.5%) had at least one comorbidity; the most common comorbidities were hypertension and hyperlipidemia (18.2%; Table 1). There was no significant difference between the long COVID and PCON groups in terms of age, sex, BMI, and presence of comorbidity (Table 1). In addition, we surveyed patients with long COVID on the basis of a questionnaire about the extent to which 31 long COVID-related symptoms had worsened (Supplementary Table S1). Similar to a previous report [11], patients with long COVID in this study showed one or more worsened symptoms, such as fatigue (48.4%), memory impairment (48.4%), loss of energy (41.9%), visual disturbance (38.7%), and concentration impairment (32.3%; Fig. 1).Fig. 1
Symptoms experienced by patients with long COVID. Values are presented as total number of each clinical outcome obtained from PROs

To identify factors related to long COVID symptoms, we analyzed the correlation of the surveyed scores with their clinical characteristics. The severity of long COVID symptoms, based on the total score of surveyed symptoms, was higher in the patients who needed hospitalization during COVID-19 development (severe group) than in those who received treatment at home or a treatment center during initial COVID-19 development (mild group; Fig. 2A). The severity of long COVID symptoms also showed a significant positive correlation with the level of serum triglycerides (r = 0.6804, p = 0.0249; Fig. 2B). Moreover, when correlations between categorized symptom scores and biomarkers were analyzed, the serum level of fasting blood glucose positively correlated with the severity of cardiopulmonary symptoms (r = 0.6935, p = 0.0106). The level of high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol showed a negative correlation with neurological (r = −0.7113, p = 0.0375) and psychological (r = −1.000, p = 0.0167) symptoms (Fig. 2C).Fig. 2
Associations between clinical data and severity of long COVID symptoms. A Effect of the severity of initial COVID-19 development on the severity of long COVID symptoms. Results are presented as mean ± standard error of the mean (SEM). Significant differences were determined using Mann–Whitney U test, **p < 0.01 B Triglyceride levels significantly correlated with total long COVID symptoms. C Correlations between blood markers and categorized long COVID symptoms, as determined via Spearman’s rank correlation (Spearman’s correlation coefficient, r, and p-value). Each dot represents an individual participant

sec.title: Gut Microbiome and Bacterial Markers Associated with Long COVID Progression
sec.p:
To compare the gut microbial composition among the NCON, PCON, and long COVID groups, we analyzed participant’s feces on the basis of 16S rRNA amplicon (V3–V4 region) sequencing. The α-diversity in the long COVID group did not significantly differ from that in other groups. PCONs showed reduced species richness compared with NCONs, according to the Chao 1 index (Fig. 3A). However, there were no significant differences in the Simpson and Shannon indices (Fig. 3A). Although PCONs or patients with long COVID exhibited no significant difference compared with NCONs in terms of β-diversity, there was a significant dissimilarity between patients with long COVID and PCONs at the community level (Supplementary Fig. S1A and 3B).Fig. 3
Patients with long COVID have distinct microbial profiles compared with control groups. The gut microbiomes of negative SARS-CoV-2 controls (NCONs, n = 23), positive SARS-CoV-2 controls (PCONs, n = 14), and patients with long COVID (long COVID, n = 31) were analyzed on the basis of 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing. A Violin plots showing α-diversity of the NCON, PCON, and long COVID groups (Chao 1, Simpson, and Shannon indices). B Non-metric multidimensional scaling (MDS) plot showing β-diversity between the PCON and long COVID. C Potentially different microbes between the PCON and long COVID groups, as deduced using random forest analysis, at the genus level. D Comparison of the relative abundance of bacterial genera between the PCON and long COVID groups. Each dot represents an individual participant. E Comparison of microbiome composition between PCON and long COVID using ANCOM-BC analysis. F Receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC) of each genus useful for distinguishing between the long COVID and PCON groups (upper panel), along with the area under the curve (AUC) and 95% confidence interval (lower panel), respectively. Mean values are displayed as lines. Significant differences were determined using Kruskal–Wallis test followed by Dunn’s multiple comparisons test (A), permutational analysis of variance (PERMANOVA) (B), Mann–Whitney U test (D), and ANCOM-BC analysis (E; q-value, false discovery rate (FDR)-corrected). *p or q < 0.05, **p or q < 0.01, and ***p or q < 0.001

To determine the bacterial genera that differentiate patients with long COVID from PCONs, we performed random forest analysis (Fig. 3C) and then compared the relative abundance of the top-ranked genera (Fig. 3D). The gut microbiome of patients with long COVID was characterized by the enrichment of genera Leuconostoc, Actinomyces, Granulicatella, Weissella, Paludicola, Clostridium_sensu_strico_1, and Phascolarctobacterium (Fig. 3D). To rigorously address the inherently compositional nature of microbiome data, analysis of compositions of microbiomes with bias correction (ANCOM-BC) was additionally performed to compare the relative abundances between patients with long COVID and PCON. The results showed that families Peptostreptococcaceae and Lachnospiraceae and genus Leuconostoc were enriched in the patient with long COVID (Fig. 3E). Furthermore, among different genera between long COVID and PCON, the AUC values of Leuconostoc, Granulicatella, and Actinomyces in distinguishing long COVID from PCON groups were 0.885 (95% confidence interval (CI) 0.765–1.000), 0.783 (95% CI 0.633–0.933), and 0.733 (95% CI 0.555–0.911), respectively (Fig. 3F). The results suggested that these three genera could be useful as bacterial markers to predict whether COVID-19 will progress to long COVID. Similarly, we conducted a random forest analysis between the long COVID and NCON groups and compared the relative abundance of top-ranked genera (Supplementary Fig. S1B). Compared with that in the NCON group, the relative abundance of Leuconostoc and Actinomyces in the long COVID group increased, whereas that of Lachnospiraceae_UCG_010 and Sutterella decreased (Supplementary Fig. S1C). Collectively, Leuconostoc and Actinomyces should be considered to be characteristic gut microbial components of patients with long COVID.

sec.title: Correlation between the Gut Microbiota and Severity of Long COVID Symptoms
sec.p:
We compared the relative abundance of gut microbes in patients with long COVID and the scores of their long COVID symptoms. Several long COVID symptoms showed a meaningful correlation with parts of gut microbial components that were enriched in long COVID (Supplementary Table S2). For example, systemic symptoms were inversely correlated to the relative abundance of Family_XIII_AD3011_group (r = −0.6406; Fig. 4A and Supplementary Table S2). Digestive symptoms had a strong negative correlation with the relative abundance of Barnesiella, Alistipes, and Gordonibacter (r = −0.7024, −0.7001, and −0.6502, respectively; Fig. 4B and Supplementary Table S2). In addition, Subdoligranulum, Clostridium_sensu_stricto_1, Lachnospiraceae_ND3007_group, UCG-002, Ruminococcus, Monoglobus, and Eubacterium coprostanoligenes group negatively correlated with otolaryngologic symptoms (r = −0.8117, −0.7500, −0.7120, −0.7120, −0.7113, −0.7113, and −0.6945 respectively), whereas Blautia and Granulicatella positively associated with visual symptoms (r = 0.7233 and 0.6544, respectively; Fig. 4C and Supplementary Table S2).Fig. 4
Several microbes have a significant association with categorized long COVID symptoms. Correlations between several microbes and the severity of systemic (A), gastrointestinal (B), otolaryngologic (C), and visual symptoms (D), as determined via Spearman’s rank correlation (Spearman’s correlation coefficient, r, and p-value < 0.05). Each dot represents an individual participant. All microbes showing significant relationships with categorized long COVID symptoms are listed in Supplementary Table S2

We examined correlations between the relative abundance of gut microbes and blood markers in patients with long COVID. The levels of several blood markers, such as bilirubin, high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hsCRP), hemoglobin, and lymphocytes, significantly correlated with the relative abundance of specific microbes (Supplementary Table S3). For instance, the relative abundance of the genus Parabacteroides had a strong negative association with the levels of total and low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol (r = −0.6925 and −0.7563, respectively; Fig. 5A and B and Supplementary Table S3). Furthermore, the levels of basophils, a type of white blood cell, directly correlated with the relative abundance of the Eubacterium ventriosum group (r = 0.6811) and inversely correlated with the relative abundance of Rothia (r = −0.6286; Fig. 5C and Supplementary Table S3). These correlations suggest the potential role of specific gut microbes in the development of long COVID.Fig. 5
Several microbes are significantly associated with the levels of blood markers. Correlations between several microbes and levels of total cholesterol (A), LDL cholesterol (B), and basophils (C) based on Spearman’s rank correlation (Spearman’s correlation coefficient, r, and p-value < 0.05). Each dot represents an individual participant. All microbes showing significant relationships with blood biomarkers are listed in Supplementary Table S3

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In this study, we investigated the association between the gut microbiota and long COVID. We enrolled patients who were experiencing long COVID symptoms; their most common symptoms, such as fatigue, poor memory, loss of energy, blurred vision, and difficulty with concentration, have been consistently ranked high in other studies [26, 27]. Our findings also support previous research findings [28], indicating that individuals with severe initial COVID-19 development are likely to exhibit more severe long COVID symptoms than those with mild initial infection [29]. Moreover, previous reports have indicated that patients with COVID-19 are at a higher risk of cholesterol problems, including higher LDL, total cholesterol, and triglyceride levels and lower HDL cholesterol levels [30, 31], and patients with high fasting blood glucose levels have poor prognoses. Consistently, our results showed a positive correlation between triglyceride levels and total long COVID symptoms, in addition to fasting blood glucose and cardiopulmonary symptoms. Conversely, HDL cholesterol levels negatively correlated with psychological and neurological symptoms. Although our results do not clearly establish whether cholesterol dysregulation is a cause or consequence of prolonged COVID-19 symptoms, these findings indicate that the patients with long COVID in our study shared characteristics similar to those reported in previous studies on long COVID symptoms.

Our microbiome analysis showed that the gut microbial composition of patients with long COVID is different from that of participants without post-viral symptoms. Specifically, Leuconostoc, Granulicatella, and Actinomyces have been identified as biomarkers with significant predictive value for distinguishing long COVID from non-long COVID cases, with respective AUC values of 0.885, 0.783, and 0.733. These bacterial genera not only have diagnostic potential but may also play a role in the disease process. There is a positive correlation between cardiopulmonary symptoms and Actinomyces abundance as well as between visual symptoms and Granulicatella abundance, which further support their involvement in the manifestation of long COVID symptoms. Moreover, Actinomyces and Granulicatella are opportunistic pathobionts. Actinomyces is enriched in patients with immune-mediated inflammatory diseases, including Crohn’s disease, ulcerative colitis, multiple sclerosis, and rheumatoid arthritis [32, 33], and causes actinomycosis, characterized by the formation of painful abscesses in the mouth, lungs, breast, or gastrointestinal tract in immunodeficient hosts [34]. Similarly, Granulicatella is mainly found in immunocompromised patients [35] in whom it contributes to infective endocarditis pathogenesis by secreted virulent factors [36]. The relative abundance of these genera showed negative correlations with the level of basophils, a type of white blood cell that protects our body from infections. Notably, lower basophil levels are correlated with a poor COVID-19 prognosis [37]. Thus, the higher relative abundance of Actinomyces and Granulicatella in patients with long COVID suggests their potential role in the pathogenesis of persistent long COVID symptoms (not as a simple diagnostic biomarker).

We also examined the symptoms experienced by patients with long COVID using patient-reported outcome (PRO) questionnaires and then investigated the correlation between the gut microbiome and severity of long COVID symptoms. PRO questionnaires enable patients to report a wide range of health-related information, and including them in clinical trials can assist clinicians in making treatment decisions [38]. Our analysis revealed that various microbes had meaningful associations with long COVID outcomes measured via PROs. Among them, certain strains were found to have a significant correlation with extra intestinal health. For instance, the relative abundance of seven genera, including Subdoligranulum, Clostridium_sensu_stricto 1, Lachnospiraceae_ND3007 group, UCG-002, Ruminococcus, Monoglobus, and Eubacterium coprostanoligenes group, was inversely correlated with the severity of otolaryngologic symptoms. Blautia and Granulicatella abundance showed a strong positive correlation with visual symptoms. These findings suggest a potential role for specific gut bacteria in exacerbating or alleviating particular symptoms and a complex interplay between gut health and systemic health. Furthermore, the relationship between gut microbial composition and blood markers highlights the systemic effect of gut dysbiosis. For example, serum total cholesterol and LDL cholesterol levels have a negative correlation with Parabacteroides abundance. This relationship is reported to play a beneficial role in obesity and metabolic dysfunctions by improving blood dyslipidemia, including total cholesterol, LDL cholesterol, free fatty acid, and triglyceride levels [39, 40]. The role of particular strains in developing long COVID symptoms remains to be determined in future studies.

Our study has several implications for the treatment and management of long COVID. First, the identification of gut microbial signatures associated with long COVID could lead to the development of diagnostic tools for early detection of post-viral syndromes. Second, these microbial signatures could guide the development of targeted probiotic or dietary interventions to restore a healthy gut microbiota balance, potentially alleviating long COVID symptoms. Despite our promising results, the study had limitations that must be addressed in future research. First, the relatively small cohort size and variability in timing of sample collection limits the generalizability of our findings. Owing to this limitation, achieving statistical significance (q < 0.05) in correlation analyses was challenging after correcting for multiple comparisons (FDR correction), although the sample size was sufficient to detect meaningful associations (p < 0.05). Further studies with larger and more diverse cohorts that include standardized timing are necessary to validate these results and to better understand their broader applicability. Second, we only explored correlations among the gut microbiome, blood markers, and long COVID symptoms; therefore, we could not directly show whether the altered gut microbes were a cause or effect of long COVID symptoms. Direct evidence of causality through in vivo animal experiments or longitudinal studies is needed to elucidate the causality between gut microbiota changes and long COVID development. Third, our study relied on patient self-reporting of symptoms of long COVID, which may be subject to subjective bias. Although biomarker levels in the blood have been utilized, the development of disease-specific clinical parameters will be necessary to achieve more promising results. Finally, although 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing is helpful for understanding the overall composition of the microbiome, it has limitations in detecting differences at the species level. More sophisticated methods, such as shot-gun metagenomic sequencing, are needed to analyze the implications of species-level bacteria.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Overall, our results underscore the potential of the gut microbiome as a diagnostic biomarker and therapeutic target for long COVID. By advancing our understanding of the gut–lung axis and its role in post-viral syndromes, we can pave the way for more effective management strategies for long COVID and potentially similar post-viral syndromes. Further large-scale validation studies are essential to confirm the potential association between gut microbial changes and specific long COVID symptoms, which could substantially improve patient outcomes and quality of life for individuals affected by long COVID.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p:
Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material.
Supplementary File 1 (PDF 337 KB)

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.
Soyoung Oh and Sujin An have contributed equally to the study as first authors.
Donghyun Kim and Haesook Seo have contributed equally to the study as last authors.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: We would like to acknowledge the participants for taking part in this study.

notes.title: Author Contributions
notes.p: Soyoung Oh designed the study, registered the participants, collected fecal samples of NCONs and patients, performed the laboratory experiments, analyzed the data, and wrote the first draft of the manuscript. Sujin An designed the study, performed the laboratory experiments, and analyzed the data. Kihyun Park and Soohyung Lee provided the data of patients. Yoo-min Han and Seong-Joon Koh provided fecal samples and data on NCONs and PCONs. Joowon Lee and Hyerin Gim reviewed the clinical data. Haesook Seo and Donghyun Kim supervised the study design, revised the manuscript, and reviewed the final manuscript. All authors approved the final version. All authors meet the authorship criteria recommended by the International Committee of Medical Journal Editors (ICMJE). Each author made substantial contributions to the conception or design of the study; or the acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of data; participated in drafting or critically revising the manuscript; approved the final version; and agreed to be accountable for all aspects of the work. Corresponding Authors: Correspondence to Donghyun Kim and Haesook Seo.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This study was supported by a grant from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT (RS-2022-NR069587 to D.K. and RS-2023–00227939 to D.K. and S.J.K.), including coverage of the journal’s publication fee.

notes.title: Data Availability
notes.p: All raw 16S rRNA amplicon sequence data derived from this study have been submitted to NCBI BioProject under accession no. PRJNA1099312 (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/bioproject/PRJNA1099312). The other human datasets used are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request after IRB approval.

notes.title: Conflict of Interest
notes.p: Soyoung Oh, Sujin An, Kihyun Park, Soohyung Lee, Yoo-min Han, Seong-Joon Koh, Joowon Lee, Hyerin Gim, Donghyun Kim, and Haesook Seo declare that they have no competing interests.

notes.title: Ethical Approval
notes.p: This study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of the Seoul Medical Center and Seoul National University Hospital (approval nos. SEOUL 2023–02-005 and 2011–202-1178). Informed consent was obtained from all study participants. The study was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki of 1964 and its later amendments.
